walkthrough.title: Unfold the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Kingsburg set up. First, unfold the main board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach. You will see eighteen numbered advisor spaces running along the top, from the Jester at number one all the way to the Queen at number eighteen. Below those you will see the year track with five spaces and the season track.

walkthrough.title: Set the Year and Season Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the year marker and place it on Year one. Then find the season marker and place it on the Spring space. The game runs for five years, and each year has four seasons: Spring, Summer, and Autumn are productive seasons where you roll dice and visit advisors, and Winter is when enemies attack.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Enemy Deck
walkthrough.text: Now find the stack of twenty-five enemy cards. Give them a good shuffle face down. Deal one enemy card face down onto each of the five Winter spaces on the year track. Do not peek at them. Put the remaining cards back in the box without looking. These enemies will be revealed at the end of each year for the Winter battle.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all the resource cubes and sort them into three piles by type: gold, wood, and stone. Place these piles near the board where everyone can reach them. These form the general supply that players will draw from throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the King's Envoy Token
walkthrough.text: Find the King's Envoy token and set it near the board. This special token is awarded each productive season to whichever player rolls the lowest total on their dice. It gives a powerful bonus, so even rolling low has its perks.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Reinforcement Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the King's reinforcement tokens. These are small tokens that provide plus-one military strength during Winter battles. Place them in a pile near the board. Players will earn these from certain advisors and building abilities throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Plus-Two Token
walkthrough.text: Locate the white plus-two token and set it near the board as well. This token can be earned from certain advisors or buildings and lets you add two to one of your dice in a future season. It is a powerful tool for reaching high-numbered advisors.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player one building board. This personal board shows twenty buildings organized into five rows and four columns. Each building has a cost in resources, a victory point value, and sometimes a special ability or military bonus. You will be constructing these buildings over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player takes three standard dice in their chosen color. These are your workers for the entire game. Every productive season you will roll all three dice and use them to visit advisors on the main board.

walkthrough.title: Place Scoring Discs
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a scoring disc in their color and places it on zero on the score track around the edge of the main board. Victory points are tracked throughout the game, so your disc will move as you earn points from advisors, buildings, and battles.

walkthrough.title: Confirm Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Everyone starts with zero resources. No gold, no wood, no stone. Your very first productive season will be your chance to start gathering what you need. Make sure nobody accidentally grabs resources during setup.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: All players roll their three dice simultaneously. The player with the lowest total goes first in the first productive season and receives the King's Envoy token. If there is a tie for lowest, the player using fewer dice to make that total goes first. You are now ready to begin Year one.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Kingsburg is played over five years. Each year has four seasons: Spring, Summer, and Autumn are productive seasons where you roll dice and visit advisors to collect resources. Winter is when enemies attack and you must defend your province. After the Winter battle of Year five, the game ends and whoever has the most victory points wins.

walkthrough.title: Productive Season Overview
walkthrough.text: During each productive season, five things happen in order. First, everyone rolls their three dice at the same time. Second, you determine turn order based on who rolled lowest. Third, players take turns placing dice on advisor spaces to claim them. Fourth, everyone collects their advisor rewards. Fifth, each player may build one building. Let's go through each step in detail.

walkthrough.title: Rolling Dice and Turn Order
walkthrough.text: All players roll their three dice simultaneously. Add up your totals. The player with the lowest sum goes first, then the next lowest, and so on. If two players tie, the one using fewer dice to make that total goes first. The player with the lowest total also receives the King's Envoy token, which gives a special bonus we will cover shortly.

walkthrough.title: Placing Dice on Advisors
walkthrough.text: In turn order, each player places one or more of their dice on an advisor space. The key rule is that the numbers on the dice you place must add up exactly to the advisor's number. For example, to visit Advisor seven, you could place a single seven, or a three and a four, or a one, two, and four. Each advisor can only be claimed by one player per season, so if someone takes the advisor you wanted, you need a backup plan.

walkthrough.title: Multiple Advisor Visits
walkthrough.text: You do not have to spend all your dice on a single advisor. If you place one die on Advisor three and another on Advisor five, that uses two dice on two different advisors. Your remaining die could go on a third advisor. The only rule is that each group of dice placed together must sum exactly to the advisor's number, and you must place all three dice somewhere each season.

walkthrough.title: Collecting Advisor Rewards
walkthrough.text: After all players have placed their dice, everyone collects rewards from their advisors in ascending numerical order. This means Advisor one pays out first, then two, then three, and so on. Lower-numbered advisors give modest rewards like one gold or one wood. Higher-numbered advisors give larger combinations of resources, military strength, and victory points. Advisor eighteen, the Queen, gives two gold, two wood, two stone, and one victory point.

walkthrough.title: The Three Resource Types
walkthrough.text: There are three resource types in Kingsburg: gold, wood, and stone. You collect them from advisors and spend them to construct buildings. Different buildings require different combinations, so you want to gather the right mix for the buildings you plan to construct. Resources carry over between seasons and years, so there is no penalty for saving up.

walkthrough.title: Building Phase
walkthrough.text: After collecting rewards, each player may construct one building from their personal building board by paying its resource cost. Remove the required gold, wood, and stone from your supply and place a marker on that building. Buildings give you victory points, military strength, special abilities, or some combination. You can only build one building per productive season, so choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Building Board Progression
walkthrough.text: Your building board has five rows and four columns. Within each column, you must build from the bottom row upward. You cannot build a Row two building until the Row one building in that same column is complete. This means you need to plan your building path early. Higher rows contain more powerful and expensive buildings, so reaching them takes deliberate planning over multiple years.

walkthrough.title: The King's Envoy
walkthrough.text: The King's Envoy is awarded to the lowest roller each productive season. It gives you a choice of two powerful bonuses. First option: you may build a second building during the building phase, letting you construct two buildings in one season. Second option: you may place dice on an advisor space that is already claimed by another player, ignoring the one-player-per-advisor rule. This makes rolling low surprisingly valuable.

walkthrough.title: The Plus-Two Token
walkthrough.text: Certain advisors and building abilities grant you the plus-two token. When you have this token, you may add two to one of your dice during a future season's placement phase. This effectively turns a die showing four into a six, for example. It is a one-time-use bonus that can help you reach otherwise impossible high-numbered advisors. After using it, return it to the supply.

walkthrough.title: Winter Battle Phase
walkthrough.text: At the end of each year, after the three productive seasons, Winter arrives. Reveal the face-down enemy card for that year. Each enemy has a strength number that you must meet or exceed. Your military strength comes from the total military bonus on your constructed buildings, plus any King's reinforcement tokens you have earned, plus a one-die roll made during Winter.

walkthrough.title: Battle Outcomes
walkthrough.text: There are three possible outcomes in a Winter battle. If your military strength meets or exceeds the enemy strength, you win and gain the listed reward, which is usually victory points or resources. If your strength is lower, you lose and suffer a penalty, which can mean losing resources, victory points, or even having a building destroyed. If your strength exactly ties the enemy, nothing happens, no reward and no penalty.

walkthrough.title: Escalating Enemy Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Enemies get tougher each year. Year one enemies are relatively weak, but by Year four and Year five, the enemies can be devastating. Losing a battle in the late game can undo several turns of building progress. This means you must invest in military strength throughout the game, especially in the middle years, to prepare for the toughest fights.

walkthrough.title: Special Building Abilities
walkthrough.text: Some buildings grant ongoing abilities that trigger every season or every year. These include free resources at the start of certain seasons, the ability to reroll dice, extra military strength, and bonus victory points. Buildings with ongoing abilities are especially valuable in the early game because their benefits compound over the remaining years.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the Winter battle of Year five. Your final score is the total of all victory points accumulated during the game from advisors, battle victories, and building effects, plus the victory points printed on your constructed buildings, plus a bonus of one victory point for every three leftover resources you have in any combination. The player with the highest total wins. Ties are broken by most total resources remaining, then most buildings constructed.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Year One
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Kingsburg! We will walk through the first year with three example players to show you how everything works. Player 1 will make smart decisions, Player 2 will try different approaches, and Player 3 will make some common beginner mistakes so you can learn from them. Let's jump into Spring of Year one.

walkthrough.title: Spring: Everyone Rolls
walkthrough.text: All three players pick up their three dice and roll them at the same time. Player 1 rolls a two, three, and four for a total of nine. Player 2 rolls a one, five, and six for a total of twelve. Player 3 rolls a three, three, and five for a total of eleven. Player 1 has the lowest total, so Player 1 goes first and receives the King's Envoy token.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Smart Dice Splitting
walkthrough.text: Player 1 decides to split their dice across multiple advisors instead of dumping all three on one space. They place the four on Advisor four, getting one wood and one gold. Then they place the two and three together on Advisor five, getting one stone. That is two advisor visits in one season and a nice mix of all three resource types.

walkthrough.title: Why Splitting Was Smart
walkthrough.text: By visiting two advisors, Player 1 collected three total resources instead of the two they might have gotten from a single mid-range advisor. In the early game, quantity of resources usually matters more than hitting one big advisor. Splitting also gives you more flexibility since no single blocked advisor ruins your whole turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Going for a High Advisor
walkthrough.text: Player 2 decides to put all three dice on Advisor twelve, which requires a sum of twelve. They use their one, five, and six to get there. Advisor twelve gives a strong reward with multiple resources. This is a valid strategy, but it means Player 2 only visits one advisor this season and has no flexibility.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Resource Balance
walkthrough.text: Player 3 places all their dice to maximize gold, visiting advisors that give only gold rewards. They end up with three gold and nothing else. The problem is that most buildings require a mix of wood, stone, and gold. Player 3 cannot build anything useful this turn because they are missing wood and stone entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Your Resources
walkthrough.text: Don't chase a single resource type. Look at the buildings you want to construct and work backward to figure out what mix of resources you need. Most Row one buildings cost a combination of two or three resource types. If you hoard one type, you will waste entire seasons unable to build while opponents pull ahead.

walkthrough.title: Spring Building Phase
walkthrough.text: Now everyone collects their rewards in ascending advisor order and then has the chance to build. Player 1 uses their wood, gold, and stone to build a Row one building right away. Player 2 builds a different Row one building with their haul from Advisor twelve. Player 3 looks at their pile of gold and realizes they cannot afford anything useful yet.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Uses the King's Envoy
walkthrough.text: Remember, Player 1 has the King's Envoy from rolling lowest. They choose to use it to build a second building this phase. That is two buildings in one season while everyone else built one or zero. The King's Envoy is incredibly valuable in the early game for this reason, so do not feel bad about rolling low.

walkthrough.title: Summer Season Begins
walkthrough.text: The season marker advances to Summer. Everyone rolls dice again. This time Player 3 rolls lowest with a total of seven. Player 3 gets the King's Envoy and goes first. Let's see if Player 3 learns from their Spring mistake.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Always Chasing the Queen
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has a total of seven, which is not enough to reach the high-value advisors. Instead of working with what they have, they complain about their low roll and grudgingly places dice on low advisors without thinking strategically. They forget that they have the King's Envoy, which could let them build two buildings or share a taken advisor space.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Low Rolls Are Not Bad
walkthrough.text: New players often think low rolls are terrible, but they are not. Going first means you get first pick of all eighteen advisors with no competition. The King's Envoy is a massive bonus that can let you build two buildings in one turn. Two or three low advisors often give as many total resources as one high advisor. Embrace the low roll.

walkthrough.title: Autumn: The Last Productive Season
walkthrough.text: The season advances to Autumn. This is your last chance to gather resources before Winter arrives. Everyone rolls and places dice on advisors one more time. By now each player should have built one or two buildings. Think about what resources you still need to build before Winter, and also consider whether you have enough military strength to face the first enemy.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Forgetting About Winter
walkthrough.text: Player 3 spent all of Autumn grabbing resources to build an expensive economic building and completely forgot about the upcoming Winter battle. They have zero military strength from buildings and no reinforcement tokens. Even though Year one enemies are relatively weak, Player 3 is in serious danger of losing the battle and suffering a penalty.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Plan for Winter
walkthrough.text: Never forget that Winter is coming at the end of every year. Check the military strength on the buildings you are constructing. Even a small amount of military from Row one buildings can make the difference between winning and losing the Year one battle. Ignoring military entirely is one of the biggest beginner mistakes in Kingsburg.

walkthrough.title: Winter Battle: Year One
walkthrough.text: Winter arrives. Flip over the enemy card for Year one. Let's say the enemy has a strength of three. Each player adds up their military from buildings, reinforcement tokens, and rolls one die. Player 1 has two military from buildings plus rolls a three, totaling five. They win easily and gain the victory reward. Player 2 has one military plus rolls a four, totaling five. They win too.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 Faces the Consequences
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has zero military from buildings, no reinforcement tokens, and rolls a two. Their total military is just two against an enemy strength of three. Player 3 loses the battle and must suffer the penalty on the enemy card, which might mean losing a resource or even a victory point. It is a small loss in Year one, but this pattern will be devastating in later years when enemies are much stronger.

walkthrough.title: Year One Complete
walkthrough.text: Year one is over. The year marker advances to Year two. The season marker resets to Spring. Take a moment to look at your building board and plan your strategy for the next year. What buildings do you want to construct next? Do you need more military before the Year two battle? Which columns are you progressing up?

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Redemption
walkthrough.text: In Year two, Player 3 takes the lessons to heart. They split their dice across two advisors for a balanced mix of resources, build a Row one building that gives military strength, and even remember to use the King's Envoy when they roll lowest. When the Year two Winter battle arrives, Player 3 has enough military to win. Smart play.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have played through your first year of Kingsburg and seen the key flow of the game. You know how to roll, place dice on advisors, collect resources, build buildings, and fight Winter battles. You have also seen three critical beginner mistakes: hoarding one resource type, undervaluing low rolls, and ignoring military. You are ready to keep playing.

walkthrough.title: Before You Rule
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full five-year game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common mistakes new players make.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all eighteen advisors and building terminology.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Rule
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick guide to advisors and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up almost every new player. First, hoarding one resource type and being unable to build for multiple seasons. Second, always dumping all three dice on one high advisor instead of considering splits. Third, completely ignoring military until Year three when the enemies suddenly become brutal. Fourth, forgetting to use the King's Envoy when you roll low. Fifth, not building every season when you have the resources to do so. Keep these in mind and you will be ahead of most first-time players.

walkthrough.title: Split Versus Stack Your Dice
walkthrough.text: One of the most important decisions each season is whether to place all three dice on one advisor or split them across two or three. Stacking all three dice lets you reach the high-numbered advisors with massive rewards, but you only get one visit. Splitting gives you multiple smaller rewards that often add up to more total resources. As a general rule, split your dice in the early game when you need a variety of resources to build. Stack them in the late game when you need one specific big reward to finish a key building or hit a military threshold.

walkthrough.title: The King's Envoy Is Your Friend
walkthrough.text: New players groan when they roll low, but the King's Envoy is one of the strongest tools in the game. Building two buildings in a single season accelerates your engine enormously, especially in Years one and two when cheap Row one buildings are available. The alternative use, sharing an advisor space that was already claimed, is great for blocking opponents or grabbing a crucial resource. Always have a plan for how to use the Envoy before you start placing dice.

walkthrough.title: Military Investment Timing
walkthrough.text: Military is easy to ignore because it only matters once per year during Winter. But losing a battle in Years four or five can cost you a building you spent the entire game working toward. The sweet spot is to have some military from Row one buildings by the end of Year one, then steadily increase through Years two and three. By Year four, you want enough base military that even a mediocre die roll wins the battle. Do not try to cram all your military investment into Year four. By then it is usually too late.

walkthrough.title: Build Every Season If Possible
walkthrough.text: Unused resources are wasted tempo. If you have enough resources to build something at the end of a productive season, you almost always should. Even if the building available is not your top priority, it unlocks the next row in that column and adds victory points or abilities to your board. The only exception is when you are saving resources for a specific expensive building you plan to construct next season. Skipping a build without a clear plan is one of the most common ways new players fall behind.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Building Path
walkthrough.text: Your building board has four columns, and you must build from bottom to top within each column. Before the game even starts, glance at the Row four and Row five buildings and decide which columns have the most appealing end goals. Then make sure you are building the prerequisite lower-row buildings in those columns first. A common mistake is building whatever is cheapest each turn without thinking about which higher-row buildings those unlock. The players who win are the ones who plan their path two or three buildings ahead.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Kingsburg has real player interaction through the advisor spaces. If you notice an opponent consistently targeting certain advisors, consider blocking them by placing there first. Similarly, if you see someone hoarding resources for a big building, you might want to claim the advisors that give the resources they need. The King's Envoy is the ultimate counter-play tool since it lets you share an already-claimed advisor. Pay attention to what other players are building and which advisors they need.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Your Dice
walkthrough.text: You cannot control what you roll, so flexibility is key. Before every season, think about which two or three advisor combinations would work well for different roll outcomes. If you roll high, consider one big advisor or an efficient split. If you roll low, take advantage of the King's Envoy and first pick of all advisors. The worst thing you can do is fixate on one perfect plan and then panic when the dice do not cooperate. Have two or three backup plans ready and you will always make the most of your rolls.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't hoard one resource type — most buildings need a mix
Don't always stack all 3 dice on one advisor — splits often yield more
Don't ignore military until Year 3+ — enemies escalate fast
Don't forget the King's Envoy when you roll low
Build every season if you can — unused resources = wasted tempo

summary.title: Split Versus Stack Your Dice
bullets: Split dice early game for resource variety → build sooner
Stack dice late game for one big targeted reward
2-3 small advisors often beat 1 big advisor in total resources
Splitting reduces risk of being blocked

summary.title: The King's Envoy Is Your Friend
bullets: Low roll = first pick of all 18 advisors + King's Envoy
Envoy option A: build 2 buildings in one season
Envoy option B: share an already-claimed advisor
Especially powerful in Years 1-2 with cheap Row 1 buildings

summary.title: Military Investment Timing
bullets: Some military from Row 1 buildings by end of Year 1
Steadily increase through Years 2-3
By Year 4, base military should survive a mediocre die roll
Don't cram all military into Year 4 — too late by then

summary.title: Build Every Season If Possible
bullets: Unused resources = wasted tempo
Even a low-priority building unlocks the next row in that column
Only skip building if saving for a specific expensive building next season
Skipping builds without a plan is a common way to fall behind

summary.title: Plan Your Building Path
bullets: 4 columns, must build bottom-to-top within each
Look at Row 4-5 buildings and work backward to plan prerequisites
Don't just build whatever is cheapest — think 2-3 buildings ahead
The best paths lead to high-VP or high-military endgame buildings

summary.title: Watch Your Opponents
bullets: Block opponents by claiming advisors they need
King's Envoy counters blocks (share an already-claimed advisor)
Track what opponents are building and which resources they need
Interaction through advisor spaces is a key part of the game

summary.title: Adapt to Your Dice
bullets: Can't control rolls — flexibility is key
Plan 2-3 advisor combos before rolling for different outcomes
High roll → big advisor or efficient split
Low roll → King's Envoy + first pick of all advisors

walkthrough.title: Rush Building Strategy
walkthrough.text: The rush building strategy focuses on constructing as many buildings as possible in the first two years. Prioritize the cheapest Row one buildings across multiple columns to unlock Row two quickly. Use the King's Envoy to double-build whenever you roll low. The advantage is that building abilities compound over the remaining years. A building constructed in Year one gives its bonus for five years, while one built in Year four only helps for two. Even modest ongoing abilities become powerful with enough time to accumulate.

walkthrough.title: Military Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: Instead of building for economy, this approach invests heavily in military buildings and reinforcement tokens. The goal is to win every single Winter battle and collect the victory rewards each year. Battle rewards scale up significantly in later years, so winning Years three through five can be worth more than several buildings. The key is to grab advisors that offer military bonuses and reinforcement tokens, and to build in columns that provide military strength. Pair this with the Sergeant at Advisor five and similar military-focused advisors.

walkthrough.title: Advisor Blocking Strategy
walkthrough.text: This strategy focuses on reading your opponents and deliberately blocking them from their preferred advisors. Watch which resources opponents are hoarding and which building columns they are progressing through. Then place your dice on the advisors they need most, even if the reward is slightly suboptimal for you. This works best when you go early in turn order. Combine it with the King's Envoy ability to share already-claimed spaces when you need to access a blocked advisor yourself.

walkthrough.title: Column Specialization
walkthrough.text: Instead of spreading builds across all four columns, focus on pushing one or two columns to Row four or Row five as fast as possible. The highest-row buildings offer the best victory points and the strongest abilities. By concentrating your resources on fewer columns, you reach those powerful buildings sooner while opponents are still building across the board. The trade-off is less diversity in building abilities, but the raw victory points from Row four and five buildings often outweigh that.

walkthrough.title: Dice Probability and Advisor Targeting
walkthrough.text: Understanding dice probabilities gives you a real edge. With three dice, the most likely totals are nine, ten, and eleven, while three and eighteen are extremely rare. This means advisors in the seven to twelve range are the most contested. Plan backup advisors in the less contested ranges, particularly the low numbers where competition is lighter. Also remember that advisors are resolved in ascending order, so claiming Advisor one sometimes triggers before an opponent can use their higher advisor's ability to interfere with your plans.

walkthrough.title: Plus-Two Token Timing
walkthrough.text: The plus-two token is a one-time boost that many players use inefficiently. Save it for a season where it unlocks a critical high-numbered advisor you could not otherwise reach. The ideal use is when you roll just two short of a game-changing advisor like sixteen, seventeen, or eighteen. Do not waste it to bump from Advisor four to Advisor six when the marginal reward difference is small. Time it for maximum impact, ideally in Year three or four when the rewards from high advisors matter most for your endgame push.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Resource Conversion
walkthrough.text: In the final seasons of Year five, shift your focus from building to accumulating leftover resources. Every three resources convert to one victory point at game end, regardless of type. If you cannot build anything meaningful with your remaining seasons, grab resource-heavy advisors and stockpile. This is especially effective if you have already built your key buildings and have no useful construction left. Three or four extra victory points from resource conversion can make the difference in a close game.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Player Count
walkthrough.text: Kingsburg plays differently at two players versus five. With fewer players, advisor spaces are less contested, so you can reliably target specific advisors each season. Focus on optimizing your own engine. With more players, blocking becomes fierce and flexibility matters more. Have multiple advisor plans ready each season because your first choice will often be taken. The King's Envoy also increases in value at higher player counts because the ability to share a claimed advisor overcomes the increased competition.

summary.title: Rush Building Strategy
bullets: Build as many Row 1 buildings as possible in Years 1-2
Use King's Envoy to double-build when rolling low
Building abilities compound — Year 1 build gives bonus for 5 years
Unlock Row 2+ across multiple columns early

summary.title: Military Dominance Strategy
bullets: Invest heavily in military buildings + reinforcement tokens
Win every Winter battle — rewards scale up in later years
Years 3-5 battle rewards can exceed several buildings' VP
Target Advisor 5 (Sergeant) and military-focused advisors

summary.title: Advisor Blocking Strategy
bullets: Read opponents: track their resources and building columns
Block advisors they need, even if suboptimal for you
Works best when going early in turn order
Use King's Envoy to access your own blocked advisors

summary.title: Column Specialization
bullets: Focus on 1-2 columns → push to Row 4-5 ASAP
Row 4-5 buildings have best VP and strongest abilities
Concentrated resources reach top rows faster than spreading
Trade-off: less ability diversity, more raw VP

summary.title: Dice Probability and Advisor Targeting
bullets: Most likely 3-dice totals: 9, 10, 11 (most contested advisors)
Plan backups in less contested ranges (low and very high)
Advisors resolve ascending — low advisors trigger first
3 and 18 are extremely rare totals

summary.title: Plus-Two Token Timing
bullets: Save for a critical high advisor (16, 17, 18) you barely miss
Don't waste on small marginal gains (4 → 6)
Best timing: Year 3-4 for endgame push
One well-timed use can swing 3-5 VP

summary.title: Endgame Resource Conversion
bullets: Final Year 5 seasons: stockpile resources if no useful builds left
Every 3 leftover resources = 1 VP (any combo)
Resource-heavy advisors in final turns = free VP
3-4 extra VP from conversion can decide close games

summary.title: Adapting to Player Count
bullets: Fewer players: less blocking, optimize your own engine
More players: flexibility > optimization, have backup plans
King's Envoy value increases with player count
At 5 players, expect your first-choice advisor to be taken often

entries.definition: Rewards one gold. If no other player also chose the Jester this season, gain one additional victory point. A solid low-die option that sometimes gives a free VP bonus.
Rewards one wood. A straightforward low-number advisor useful for getting wood when you only have a single low die to spare.
Rewards one wood or one gold, your choice. Flexibility makes this advisor handy when you need to fill a specific resource gap.
Rewards one wood and one gold. Two resources from one advisor visit makes this a solid target for a single die showing four.
Rewards one stone or plus-one military strength. A key advisor for players pursuing a military strategy or needing stone for buildings.
Lets you transmute resources (trade any two for any one) and also grants one gold. Great for converting surplus resources into what you actually need.
Mid-range advisors offering increasingly valuable combinations of wood, stone, and gold. These are the most commonly contested range since the average three-die total is around ten.
Upper-mid advisors granting multiple resources plus military strength or victory points. Require at least two dice for most players and offer strong, well-rounded rewards.
High-tier advisors providing large resource hauls plus victory points. Typically require all three dice, meaning you can only visit one advisor that season.
The most powerful advisor. Rewards two gold, two wood, two stone, and one victory point. Requires a dice total of exactly eighteen, which means three sixes or creative use of the plus-two token.
One of the three resource types, represented by gold cubes. Used in combination with wood and stone to construct buildings. Commonly available from lower-numbered advisors.
One of the three resource types, represented by brown cubes. Required for most buildings. Available from many advisors across the numbering range.
One of the three resource types, represented by gray cubes. Often the scarcest resource, required for many mid-tier and high-tier buildings. Fewer advisors offer stone compared to gold and wood.
At game end, every three leftover resources in any combination convert to one victory point. Resources of different types can be mixed freely for this conversion.

The ability to trade any two resources for any one resource of your choice. Available through the Alchemist advisor (number six) and certain building abilities. Helps convert surplus into what you need.

Each player's personal board showing twenty buildings in five rows and four columns. Buildings must be constructed bottom-to-top within each column. Each building shows its resource cost, VP value, and any special ability.

The cheapest buildings on the player board. They provide modest VP and basic abilities like small military bonuses or minor resource generation. Must be built first to unlock higher rows in each column.
Intermediate buildings offering better VP and stronger abilities. They require Row 1 buildings as prerequisites and cost more resources. Good targets for Years two and three.

The most powerful and expensive buildings on the player board. They offer high VP, strong military, and game-changing abilities. Reaching these by Years four or five requires deliberate column planning.

Some buildings grant ongoing bonuses when constructed. These include free resources at the start of specific seasons, dice rerolls, extra military strength, and bonus VP per season. Early-game ability buildings compound over more years.

Within each of the four columns on the building board, you must build from Row 1 upward. You cannot skip rows. Planning which columns to advance is one of the most important strategic decisions in the game.

A special token awarded to the player who rolls the lowest total each productive season. Grants one of two bonuses: build a second building during the building phase, or place dice on an advisor already claimed by another player.

A one-time-use token that adds two to one of your dice during a future placement phase. Earned from certain advisors or building abilities. Best saved for reaching critical high-numbered advisors in mid-to-late game.
Tokens providing plus-one military strength during Winter battles. Earned from certain advisors and building abilities. They accumulate across years and can make the difference in close battles.

The fourth season of each year. An enemy card is revealed with a strength value. Your military (buildings plus tokens plus one die roll) is compared. Win to gain rewards, lose to suffer penalties, tie for no effect.

Twenty-five cards (five per year) placed face-down during setup, one per Winter. Each shows an enemy with a strength value and the rewards for winning and penalties for losing. Enemies grow stronger in later years.
Your combat power during Winter battles, calculated as the sum of military bonuses from constructed buildings, plus King's reinforcement tokens, plus a single die roll made during the battle phase.
The benefit gained when your military meets or exceeds the enemy's strength. Rewards vary by enemy card and typically include VP, resources, or both. Later-year rewards are significantly larger.
The punishment suffered when your military falls below the enemy's strength. Penalties vary by enemy card and can include losing resources, VP, or even having a constructed building destroyed.

Spring, Summer, and Autumn of each year. During productive seasons, players roll dice, place them on advisors, collect resources, and build. There are fifteen productive seasons in a full game (three per year across five years).
One full cycle of four seasons: Spring, Summer, Autumn, and Winter. The game lasts five years. Enemies escalate in strength each year, and resources/buildings carry over between years.

Determined each productive season by dice totals. Lowest total goes first. Ties broken by fewest dice used to make that total. Going first means first pick of advisors but often means lower-value advisors.

The core mechanic of Kingsburg. You place one, two, or three dice on an advisor whose number matches the dice sum. Each advisor can be claimed by only one player per season. You must place all three dice each season.

The track around the edge of the main board where scoring discs move throughout the game. VP is tracked in real time from advisors, battles, and building effects. Final scoring adds building VP and resource conversion.
Victory points printed on each constructed building. Added to your total during final scoring. Higher-row buildings generally have more VP printed on them.
Victory points accumulated during play from advisor rewards, Winter battle victories, and building abilities. Tracked on the score track as they are earned.
